title: Boundary $0/π$ logical subspace and bulk dynamical probes in flux-controlled anomalous Floquet quantum walks
authors: WeiCheng Ning, YanSheng Liu, XiaoXue Zhang, XiZheng Zhang
abstract: We formulate a one-dimensional flux-controlled anomalous Floquet quantum walk and show that it admits a direct microscopic realization in a driven bipartite lattice. The walk consists of a coin-dependent drift step and a momentum-dependent coin mixing step, so the same evolution operator governs quasienergy bands, boundary modes, and bulk dynamics in real space. Because the walk is chiral, the quasienergy gaps at $0$ and $π/T$ carry independent topological information, which organizes trivial, $0$-only, $π$-only, and coexistence sectors in the $(M,φ)$ plane. In the coexistence sector, a $0$ mode and a $π$ mode reside on the same edge and span a natural boundary logical subspace. One Floquet period acts there as a relative phase operation and produces a clear $2T$ response in local boundary observables. In the bulk, the same anomalous Floquet structure is probed dynamically in two complementary ways. Frame-resolved mean chiral displacements approach the two winding numbers in the clean pre-reflection window of the symmetric time frames, while selected benchmark cuts at a representative $0$ gap closing and a representative $π$ gap closing exhibit distinct local stroboscopic responses, with the $π$ gap benchmark showing a much stronger odd-even alternation. The boundary logical subspace and the bulk dynamical probes are therefore organized within one flux-controlled anomalous Floquet quantum walk, suggesting a symmetry-protected route to quantum-walk information primitives in driven microstructured lattices.
summary: This paper shows how a flux-controlled quantum walk can host a protected 0/pi logical subspace at its boundaries while allowing the bulk topology to be read out via dynamical probes. It identifies a unique doubled-period response in the coexistence sector and distinguishes between different topological gaps using local stroboscopic signatures. This suggests a robust route for implementing quantum-walk-based information primitives in driven microstructured lattices.
main_problem: Investigating whether a single microscopic family of driven lattices can simultaneously support a boundary logical subspace (0/pi edge qubit) and detectable bulk topological diagnostics via a flux-controlled protocol.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that a flux-controlled anomalous Floquet quantum walk provides a symmetry-protected boundary logical subspace with a doubled-period (2T) response and that the bulk topology can be dynamically resolved through frame-resolved mean chiral displacements.
method: The study uses momentum-space Bloch spinors, Taylor expansions around critical gap-closing points, and numerical simulations of both periodic and open boundary condition chains.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional, chiral, flux-controlled anomalous Floquet quantum walk realized in a driven bipartite lattice with a coin-dependent drift step and a momentum-dependent mixing step.
key_observables: Boundary local observables (site-one and boundary window projectors), frame-resolved mean chiral displacement, and local stroboscopic return probability.
important_parameters: Mass parameter (M), flux phase (phi), hopping amplitudes (t1, t2), and driving period (T).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a clean system (pre-reflection window) and uses small-parameter expansions near gap-closing points.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the bulk topological phase diagram in the (M, phi) plane; Figure 2 displays the quasienergy spectrum at the edge; Figure 4 compares the return probability and stroboscopic response at the 0-gap and pi-gap benchmarks.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the microscopic model, defines the topological classification and phase diagram, analyzes the boundary logical subspace dynamics, and concludes with bulk dynamical probes and gap-specific stroboscopic signatures.
why_interesting: It presents a unified framework for creating symmetry-protected quantum information primitives (edge qubits) and provides specific dynamical signatures for detecting anomalous Floquet topology in driven lattice systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Bulk topological phase diagram of the Floquet quantum walk for t1 = t2 = 1 and T = 2. Panels (a) and (b) show W1 and W2 evaluated on a high-resolution grid in the (M, ϕ) plane. The representative parameter sets used later in Figs. 2, 3, and 4 are written directly on the phase map. Circular markers denote the open boundary points Pj, square markers denote the frame-resolved bulk points Qj, and diamond markers denote the critical benchmarks C0 and Cπ. Every later numerical panel can therefore be traced back to a definite point in the bulk diagram.

FIG. 2. Open-boundary edge spectrum and boundary dynamics. Panel (a) shows the quasienergy spectrum at P4 under open boundary conditions. The color scale gives the left edge weight in a fixed boundary window, and the highlighted markers identify the left edge states near 0 and π/T on the same boundary. Panel (b) shows the site-one probability P1(m) defined in Eq. (39) for the four points P1 to P4 defined in Fig. 1, with the initial state |Ψ(0)⟩= |1, A⟩. Panel (c) shows the boundary window probability Pe(m) defined in Eq. (39) for the same four points. Panel (d) shows the optimized doubled-period response at P4 for the phase matched state in Eq. (52). The continuous trace is P1(m) itself,...

FIG. 3. Frame-resolved mean chiral displacement for the four representative bulk points Q1 to Q4 defined in Eq. (34). Panel (a) shows −2C1(m) in symmetric time frame 1 and panel (b) shows −2C2(m) in symmetric time frame 2. The dotted horizontal guides mark the quantized targets associated with the winding numbers. Over the pre-reflection time window the curves approach (W1, W2) = (0, 0) for the trivial point, (1, 1) for the 0-only point, (1, −1) for the π-only point, and (2, 0) for the coexistence point. The two-gap bulk topology is therefore dynamically resolved in the two symmetric time frames.

FIG. 4. Selected benchmark bulk critical dynamics for the two points defined in Eq. (35). Panels (a) and (b) show the return probability Pret(m) defined in Eq. (61) for the representative 0 gap benchmark C0 = (0, π/2) and the representative π gap benchmark Cπ = (π −2, 0). Panel (c) separates the even and odd subsequences for both benchmarks over the same short time window and compares the local stroboscopic fingerprints directly. The odd and even splitting is much stronger for the π gap benchmark, consistent with the leading expansion in Eq. (65). The benchmark figure is therefore focused entirely on the local stroboscopic evidence for the distinction between the two representative critical...

Spin-momentum entanglement.note: The paper's central mechanism relies on a momentum-dependent mixing step and a coin-dependent drift in a quantum walk, which directly implements entanglement between the internal (coin) and external (position/momentum) degrees of freedom.
